comment_content:
3 assignments and 1 final project. The first assignment is making a mash-up of the audio from one source with the video of another. The second is using green-screen techniques to combine characters from different media into one setting. The third is making a music video mashup incorporating elements from multiple songs or remixing a given song. The final project is basically do whatever you want, there are no rules (except be really creative). Each submission requires a 1-2 minute video of your work, and a corresponding 1-2 minute commentary video describing your creative and technical choices (except the final project, which is 2-3 minutes for both the video + commentary). You can work on the video yourself or in groups of 2 people (if you want to have a more manageable workload highly recommend you find someone to work with).

I can totally see people submitting the same types of content for all 4 assignments, as the criteria for all of them were pretty loose and are generally quite friendly towards whatever your passions or interests are.
comment_teaching:
A course that rewards those who self-learns everything and/or already have a developed hobby in music and video making.

Almost no in-class teaching was done. Prof. Horner does enthusiastically go over other people's creations (from this semester + previous semesters) in class and gives out a lot of artistic and a few technical suggestions, but these largely do not really help that much beyond video ideation. The comments he gives you after you submit your video are all just positive remarks like "Great techniques!" or "Amazing idea!", don't expect to receive actual feedback for your video (courtesy of the TA's) until after your video is shown and peer-reviewed.

Other people previously claimed that there were tutorials provided which help students who don't have that much experience get started. My opinion is that this is only to a small extent; these tutorials aren't really that helpful if you want to really impress them and get a decent grade, they either just suggest a few techniques and tools or they're way too esoteric for most people to understand, and many of them really are only for one purpose (ex. they provided a lot of deepfake AI tool tutorials for the final project, which wouldn't be that useful if you don't want to do something like that). Especially noteworthy if you aren't coming from a field or profession where sound/video design is researched or video editing is used, or you aren't a graphics design major at heart, most of the tutorials also use apps and features which either are pretty finicky to use or turns out are behind a paywall. In fact, in my opinion it is really difficult to obtain a decent grade in this course without spending several hundred dollars on video and audio editing software/AI tools (ex. video editing tools: Final Cut Pro is a one-off 2300 HKD purchase, the standard subscription for Capcut premium is 60 HKD/month which you have to purchase to do literally anything (seriously, even splitting audio and video from a clip requires Capcut premium), Davinci Resolve is free but most advanced techniques besides plugins require a subscription that costs 230 HKD/month, the full range of adobe software requires at least a 400 HKD/month subscription to use etc., iMovie is free to use if you have a Mac but I do NOT recommend it for this course as it is so basic and there are so little features).

Of course, you could have a really creative premise that compensates a lack of technology with either a really good storyline or clever artistic choices, but this is HARD to do especially without making it look or sound like it's "low-budget".

As I am only taking this to complete my last Broadening CC requirement and I have some basic experience in video editing (due to some random stuff i did for a university club a few years ago), the only two subscriptions I purchased was a standard plan for Runway ML (so I could survive assignment 2) and Capcut Premium, both of which I swiftly cancelled after I finished my final project (Audacity, GarageBand (for Mac users), and the base version of Davinci Resolve were free). Even then, I had to rely on my experience on playing an instrument for several years and having a decent knowledge in music theory for assignment 3 and the final project to get a good grade. I recommend you take this course if you're already passionate in video editing and music creation and have or know how to use several of the tools recommended by this course, if not RUN AWAY.
comment_workload:
4 video projects only, seems light right?

NO.

Firstly, the assignment due dates are absolutely atrocious. Assignment 1 due in the middle of add/drop period, assignment 2 right before midterm period, assignment 3 in the middle of midterm period, final project right after midterms end. I constantly found working on these videos got in the way of a ton of other stuff I was doing (for a year 4 student working on postgraduate/job applications, and I anticipate most people taking this course is year 4 given how fast COMP1943 turns from available to waitlist during the first day of course reg, this is borderline hellish).

Also, one only realizes how much effort you need to put into each video when you actually do it. Having the idea is one thing. Executing it is a whole other situation. Prof. Horner and his TA's value quantity AND quality, videos that approach the 2 (or 3) minute limit and are full of artistic details and techniques. Even if you spent a massive amount of time and energy on your assignment, or you have a really good idea, they won't judge you on your idea alone if it looks like you had trouble achieving what you wanted to do.

Don't sleep on the commentary video either, to get a higher grade its not really that suggested to just add subtitles to a running clip of your workspace, instead a separate edited video that explains every important thing you did. The first two assignments I just added subtitles to a video recording of my Capcut workspace for my commentary (because I ran out of time) and neither were received very well. Some people went above and beyond and made their commentary video seemingly more amusing and detailed than their actual assignment, especially if their original video revolves around a theme/subject that isn't very well known by many people (ex. certain sports like F1 or football), but this obviously takes a lot of time.

(also, quick PSA but please do not just throw a ton of prompts into GenAI tools and make slop videos, it gets extremely boring and repetitive after several watches)

I only spent around 8 hours working on Assignment 1, as the prompt wasn't too difficult to achieve and I already knew before the class began what I wanted to do. However, I had to spend almost 20 hours for Assignment 2 to learn Davinci Resolve, masking, green screen etc., and the TA's still thought my video was pretty mediocre. To make up for this, I spent a bit more time (~25 hours) composing and remixing the song for my Assignment 3, which paid off. Finally, I spent around 30 hours total for the final project making both my audio and video components. This course ain't for you if you don't have any free time or if you value your sleep schedule (although during midterm season most people probably don't value it anyways), I had to pull near all-nighters for these assignments.
comment_grading:
3 assignments (10%, 20%, 25%), 1 final project (35%), Peer and research ratings (10%, basically graded on completion unless they notice you're spamming)

Grade boundaries: A range was generally around 4.1-4.3, B+ range 3.5-4, B range 3-3.5, C+ range 2.5-3, C range 2-2.5. The mean generally hovered around the B-B+ range, which is actually quite good for HKUST standards, but for this course if you want A-range you need to either do something related to music or video creation you already have a lot of experience in and/or spend a huge amount of time and effort (see workload).

Also, no matter how bad your video might seem to you, send it to Prof. Horner anyways, you get a bonus if you allow your video to be shown in class and peer-reviewed.

My grades:
Assignment 1: 4.09/5
Assignment 2: 3.89/5
Assignment 3: 4.94/5
Final project: 4.90/5
Final grade: A+

comment_content:
https://course.cse.ust.hk/comp1943/

(For the rest of content please goto "Grading" session)

The course provides you free environment for video editing and appreciate each other's work. You have to edit 4 videos in one semester and your work would be shown to the whole class for grading and feedbacks.

You have to be dedicated in put your effort in every assignments and everyone could see that in the lecture. If your work is "SUPER INSPIRED", it would have a chance to be demonstrated in COMP4441 next semester as an honor.

Meanwhile, you could get inspired by different creative ideas and approaches from your classmates, TAs and the Professor throughout their feedbacks, the past work demonstrations and different student's commentary work.

Lastly, takeaway suggestions to enjoy this course (there's other suggestions in other sessions):

0. Don't be hallucinated with the 'Good grades', have a try ONLY if you're interested and willing to learn editing videos.

There's a lot of obviously disappointing works and AI slops in this semester. Some of them are even off topic and lack of efforts----even condemned as 'a waste of time' according to some classmates.

Just remember, your work would be shown in front of the class.

1. Take this course with your friend, or make a friend in this class who know video editing or even start a learning club LOL

2. Avoid free-riders, especially when your group-mate is not your friend.

3. Respect each other's work. I've seen a student trying to attack me and my friends, also disrespecting Vocaloid MVs as "boring as hell". Please have a positive attitude.
comment_teaching:
I see there's some comments claim that "There is no teaching". I have some counter-arguments.

Please remember this is not a traditional course that professor reading powerpoint slides in dead tone.
Professor Horner would bring you through all the "Best of the Past" works (with commentary) for different MIAs, and give his insights with suggestions. And after you submitted your work, Horner, TAs and your classmates would give you feedbacks that would help you for a better polished product.

Just be active, go ask Horner and TAs when you're free, when you're in need. Like in emails or right after lectures .etc. Trust me, you would learn way more than just listening to reading powerpoints.

FYI, both Professor Horner and the TAs are friendly, just don't be afraid. You could even make friends with them :D

The tutorial materials are actually available but not much being mentioned from the TAs, I hope they could mention more about this. The tutorial materials are very useful. e.g. the RVC stuff.
comment_workload:
The workload depends on these major factors:
Your current state, your expectation, your motivation, hence the dedication.

Your current state:
Do you have any basic skills for video editing?
Do you know how to have a good storytelling?
Do you have crazy ideas/inspirations in your head or in your storage? (Like pranking Horner and UST, or merging Harry Potter and UST?)
Do you have any preferred type of editing? Like meme compilation? Your own animation? Vocaloid MV? A parody or even a GenAI work?

Your expectation:
If you have a good taste and expect a "masterpiece" that's similar with the viral vids from YouTube, NicoNico and Bilibili. Please get ready to suffer from learning many skills from different YouTube tutorials. You should be ready to try and error, meanwhile looking at the deadline coming to you.

If you don't to be so 'churred', lower your bar and accept the fact that 'I can't make things perfect, but I could take my advantage'. Just choose a skillset that you're good with, like, music composition and just focus on it. Way better than start from nothing.

It would be a big red flag if don't have any expectation, your work won't be polished well if you don't push yourself.

Your motivation and dedication:
If you really love the idea and urges to execute that idea well, usually huge motivation would leverage to a MASTERPIECE. The consequence is huge amount of countless nights non-sleep.

I was amazed by one of my classmate that dedicated to personally cosplayed and filmed 星街すいせい's ビビデバ , that's a lot of effort, damn.

(I see there's a Final Year student saying "As a Year 4 student, it’s really suffering to do the project", I feel very hilarious as he delivered a very poorly-performed work and making excuses, meanwhile I'm a Year 4 student too and even having credit overload. )

It's easy to get a pass. But we all could see your effort and attitude in your video.

However, if you want to ace well. Get ready for 20+ or even 50+ hours churring for the A range or even 'high'.

For example, I had to learn Vocaloid cover, pitch tuning, After Effects .etc for my MIA3, and I made my Vocaloid Cover MV from knowing almost nothing in weeks. And getting a very good grade.

I've sacrificed 3-5 nights sleepless, dozen cans of Monsters and midnight screams.

So, manage your workload and time well, learning a new editing tool/skill requires a lot of effort and time. Please reserve at least 20+ hours per work. Add the number up if you're learning a new tool like After Effects or FL Studio or 3D rendering.
comment_grading:
In this course, you have to hand in 4 works in total, also some research ratings, here's the composition:
3 Assignments (45%: 10, 20, 25%),
Project (35%),
Peer & research ratings (10%)

The research rating would come from the research released by the TAs in Canvas, just follow the instructions and submit them on time. Then you could get easy full marks on this part.

For each assignment/project (what we called "MIA"), you had to submit:
1. a 2-3 minute work ("masterpiece")
2. a directory commentary video of your work, "includes inspirations, creative process, storytelling, video and music magic tricks, and other cool secrets." according to the source.

Your MIA's grade would come from 3 factors:
1. Professor Horner
To be honest, grading from Prof. Horner is kinda biased: he has a strong preference in so-called 'wacky-twists', in which does not favor in any really slow-paced or 'plain' work. Please have some mental preparation.
2. TAs.
3. Your peer grading:
It's done by Canvas quiz in every lecture: it would count toward your attendance so please do the quizzes.
All you have to do is to grade everyone's work out of 5 (refer to the following rubric) and leave your comments.
However, there's some students that just leave the comments empty and randomly give marks so that they could fake the attendance :(. It could strongly affect the totals marks given to everyone.
So, please do attend, enjoy the lecture, and do grade and comment your peer's work :) That would be helpful to everybody.

Marking rubric:
Wow-factor of creation video (and behind-the-scenes video), count out of 5:
5/5: Super inspired
4/5: Very inspired
3/5: Inspired
2/5: Somewhat inspired
1/5: Uninspired
0/5: Not submitted

You could either submit your work in two ways:
Plan A:
Your work would be played in class, in front of Horner, TAs and your classmates, could get a maximum of 5/5 (FULL MARK).
Strongly recommended if you want to ace the course. And you would get the feedbacks from Horner, TAs and your peers.
Plan B:  
Your work would NOT be played in class, but you could only get a MAXIMUM of 2/5. DON'T take this plan unless you just want a pass or you're too shy or your work is too bad——take a try in Plan A is always worthy.
And you would only get Horner's comment ONLY.

comment_content:
There are 3 assignments: The first one getting us familiar with video editing, the second one focusing on the use of greenscreen & the 3rd one on music remix. Then, a final project that utilizes anything and everything you've learned throughout the semester. For the lecture content, there is nothing--LITERALLY NOTHING--but watching what other students from the same course have done. (your peers & the works from previous years) Oh, and you also have to do peer reviews as well for each assignment.

Basically, just make sure you're making fun videos--everyone's review is subjective, and that includes the professor & TA as well!
comment_teaching:
There is no teaching from the professor aside from his commentary on each videos shown in class, since he believes the best way to learn is to learn from others.
Despite that, I LOVE this professor a lot! He'll patiently answer any questions you throw at him, asking him for advices and stuff. Plus, he's very lenient if you got any problems with your submissions. For one of our submissions, my group uploaded the wrong commentary & for the final project, we weren't able to finish the video in time. However, the professor was very nice about it when I emailed him.
Plus, it is very effective to "learn" from what other people did as well, as he explained what he liked & disliked from different videos each time, it's easy to understand how his grading works after the first submission--which is, effort is key!
comment_workload:
This is my own subjective opinion only, because my experience in video editing is little to none :p
The workload for this course is extremely intense, if you're not familiar with video editing beforehand, and are aiming to get good grades. For each of the submissions, you have to make a 1~2 minute video (2~3 for the final project), and a commentary video on what you have created. Basically:
-You better have some creative ideas, and don't be afraid to express them. Otherwise, picking the right topic for each video may be quite exhausting & time-consuming!
-The professor values the level of detail in your videos quite a bit, stuff like matching the BGM frame-by-frame, how you apply greenscreen/other techniques to make the video that much funnier/realistic...so if you want to get the good scores, prepare to work sleeplessly/over a few days on the videos : ) I didn't sleep AT ALL the day before each submission.
-Get yourself a teammate. The work can be done by a group of 2, so find a friend & you'll half the workload. I sure relied on my friend quite a bit, couldn't have gotten this far without my bro. (Sike, both of us still went sleepless working on it, so yeah)
-The main video is important, but the commentary may be even more important. The commentary can be a pretty fun video filled with memes or a bit more serious (like an actual commentary), but whatever your style is, you'll want to pick out some of the most important/overlooked details from the main video & explain how those moments were made, instead of just replaying the entire video with some subtitles going over it :p

TL;DR, if you want to enjoy a common core with light workload, this is NOT the place for you. If you like video editing & are willing to either spend 15~20+ hours on each assignment on average, or is OK with getting a lower grade, then this will be a fun course for ya :)
comment_grading:
You'll get in A-range as long as you're in the upper quartile after everything is evaluated. The professor & TAs seemingly grade things based on 2 aspects: creativity and how much you focused on the small details. Though, it's all subjective so your work may not be liked.

My grade (LQ/Median/HQ)
Assignment 1: 3.84/5 (3.95/4.26/4.51)
Assignment 2: 4.64/5 (3.7/4.09/4.38)
Assignment 3: 4.76/5 (3.5/4.12/4.55)
Final project: 4.81/5 (3.68/4.03/4.49)

Because they didn't like our first assignment, we adjusted our styles, put in a lot more time & effort into the subsequent submissions. We worked for about 30 hours nonstop for the final project, and at least 15~20 hours for both assignment 2 & 3 each, hence the much higher scores.

Still, the hard work paid off well for us. Me & my friend both got A+
:)

The ddl for the first job is before the add/drop period!    This is outrageous!    The other students were enjoying the first two weeks while we were catching up on our due dates.    The cost of silence forced me not to drop the course, which was a terrible decision.
In addition, if you have dreams of becoming an up主 or a youtyber, then after completing these assignments, congratulations, your dreams are most likely dashed.    From now on, you'll want to throw up when you see Pr.
In short, the workload is too much, and wouldn't get good grades.    If you take this course, you will see Hong Kong at 4 am for many days in a row.
Run away!  Run away!  Run away!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
comment_grading:
It sucks.   You have no idea what his grading criteria are.   Professors tend to smile and compliment your work in class and then give it shitty grades.
I don't know who the hell is getting the high marks.   Judging from the works shown in the class, there is no doubt that my clip can be ranked in the top 20%, but it can only get a slightly higher score than the average.
However, on each assignment, someone got more than 5 points.   I don't know who got such a score.   I've never seen anyone's work in class worthy of that grade, anyway.
My assignment was graded 4.46/4.27/4.52/4.4 and I ended up with a B+, which is nowhere near as good as others on ustspace are saying.   In short, A's are not that easy to get, if you are taking this class for a good grade, then run away, it doesn't deserve it.

comment_content:
I don't usually write course reviews but I love this course so much that I am writing a really longggg review here ^^

basically, you need to submit 5 assignments in the entire semester (so around 1 every 2-3 weeks), the first 4 will be a video around 1-2 minutes, and the last one will be a video around 2-3 minutes.

You will be editing videos using films / dramas / games / anime / anything with audios (self-composed /songs /anything). Although there are topics for each assignment, you can pretty much do anything you want with any material you like as long as it's somewhat related to the topics. For each assignment, you also need to submit a director's commentary video where you show what effort you made

I used Da Vinci Resolve, audacity and BandLab. But you can use anything you want

According to the professor and TAs, the content and format of COMP 4441 and COMP1943 is almost identical
comment_teaching:
don't expect any actual lectures on video/audio editing. TAs may do a few short tutorials on basic techniques but that's it. The professor will be playing videos edited by students in previous semesters or showing everyone's assignment and comment on what he/she did well in the assignment. Watching others' work is so much more effective to learn than having long lectures on techniques and theories in my point of view ^^

really love how he is always supportive no matter how bad a video is lol the TAs are very fun as well
comment_workload:
it depends. for example,
(i) how much effort do you want to put in each video?
- you can do a lot of edits and create a lot of new things, or you can always do really quick editing, it's entirely up to you
-> most of the time more effort = people being more impressed by your work and giving you higher marks, but if your ideas are smart, you can get a really high score (even higher than cases where people put a lot of effort but no one understands the effort)

(ii) are you interested in video/audio editing?
- I find video/audio editing really fun so I did not consider the work as workload at all. I almost treated the assignments as leisure activity lmao
- but if you hate it, I don't see why you want to get in the course in the first place
comment_grading:
I had an A+, and I think the course is a good grade course since I had no video editing experience before at all

50%- 4 assignments (all my regular assignment had marks very near the mean, one below mean and other three is slightly above mean)
40%- Final assignment (5.08/5, mean 4.2, highest 5.4)
10%- Peer evaluation (full mark for me)
10%- Listening test (bonus I think?)

For the assignments, make sure to submit them to canvas and to the prof through email. You will get a base mark no matter what if you only submit to canvas, but people who sent the prof an email will have their video shown in class and will get bonus marks (the amount of bonus will depend on what mark the prof/ta/other students gave you, but you will get some no matter what). Almost everyone would send the professor emails, so please do so as well. No one will make fun of you :)

The 10% peer evaluation is just surveys where you give marks to your peers' work. You will get the marks if you did them on time (and you didn't just give all full marks or zeros)

The listening test is not actually a test, it's more like a survey, nothing will be tested. If you didn't just submit random answers so you can finish the test asap, I think you'll be fine

tips:
(i) some reviews say audio editing is not important, I agree you can get high marks by just using a song in the background if your video is good. However, I'm sure people would be more impressed if you showed your effort to create original stuff, even if it doesn't sound professional

(ii) although of course it's important to put effort in your regular assignments, make sure to make your final assignment a really good one, since it can affect your grade hugely

(iii) you never know what the prof/ta/students like and will be impressed by, so SHOW ALL YOUR EFFORT ON THE DIRECTOR'S COMMENTARY, EVEN IF THE EFFORT SEEMS NOT WORTH MENTIONING TO YOU
-> in some sense, the director's commentary is a lot more important than your actual assignment
(especially if you did some impressive stuff, it's not surprising if some people didn't get what you did if you did not emphasize your effort in the commentary)

(iv) don't worry if you are new to editing. you will pick up things quickly when you start your first assignment

comment_content:
Basically you need to submit 5 videos (10 if you also count the director's commentary) throughout the semester (every 2-3 weeks). You can do it on your own, or work with another classmate. Also remember to do the peer review quiz each lesson.

And here are the projects we had worked on this semester:
1. Magic Mashup Trailer (remake a trailer in another style)
2. Cyberpunk: Suspended In Time (include some slow-motion video and relevant sound effects)
3. Funk’n Fight Remix (Fight scene with funky music)
4. Breakup Remix (make a breakup MV for a pair of weird character combo)
5. Final Project- Pick 1 out of 2: a. Lip Reading Music Video; b. Sequel Mashup Trailer

The first 4 regular homework "requires" you to make a video under 2 minutes, while the final one "requires" 2-3 minutes. But, as long as your video is good, exceeding the time limit is not a problem, my 4th assignment that is 2:30 long even got the highest score in class.

Here's my advice before you read the rest, make sure you are somehow interested in video/audio editing before picking it, this will hugely impact how you feel about the workload and content of this course, because video editing can be quite time-consuming and complex.

If you are inexperienced or less-experienced, but don't mind those difficulties and still want to try, you definitely have interest and should take this course, but also look out of experienced editors grabbing away your chances of getting As, their works can be close to professional. Plus, this course is not a video/audio editing course, you will have to figure out in your own.

In my opinion, people with video-editing experience have a very great headstart, those with musical skills have also quite a nice headstart too.

As you read along the reviews, you will realise the "creative sound" element seems to be quite minimal. In my opinion, yes and no. We need to express the creative sound design via video, that's why video-editing is important, and one of the important factors of video editing is sound design, so put more emphasis on this sector will help your video to be better.
comment_teaching:
The prof played videos on each lesson, showing good works from previous semesters, and also reviewing everyone's video for peer review with short comments. In some classes, the TA will give a small tutorial on using some video/audio softwares, a helpful introduction for beginners.

It's pretty much self-learning, and try your best to learn and be inspired from others; but if you have any questions, make sure to email.
comment_workload:
TL:DR-
1. You better have some interest in video/audio editing first
2.  Work with your heart, but work even more on the commentary

Some basic info about me: I am a final year, and have editing experience for 5 years because of YouTube hobby, but I haven't edited any kind of music videos until this course, and it was sure fun and expanded my editing potential. I used Davinci Resolve as the video editing software, and used Audacity to help with some audio edits. Davinci Resolve is a godsend if you are into video editing and special effects, it's free, stable and equipped with so many features.

1. This may sound condescending but 90% of students attending would be not very experienced, this had given me a huge advantage in terms of editing efficiency and quality. However, as the semester goes, some of their work have shown significant improvement, it was very exhilarating to me as an editing lover, and definitely pushed me to the limits.

So how's the workload? I would say very little. Each video I spent around 3-5 days, a total of 40 hours to finish. Now some of you may say "wow that's a lot", but this time of work is very typical for video editing, if you are going after perfection. Plus, I don't treat them as homeworks, I treat them as personal projects for fun. So ask yourself, how would you view them as? And do you have the time for that?

2. If you have the interest and the time, have fun, be creative and work it with your heart. a trick I can give you is to work even better on the director's commentary, here's why- to let the viewer better understand your work and effort, and like your work even more. My commentaries on the last two assignments were more detailed and got nearly the highest score.
comment_grading:
50%- 4 Assignments
40%- Final Assignment
10%- Peer Evaluation
10%- Listening Test (Yea, I think this is actually a bonus 10%)

For the assignments, always submit them on both canvas and email, this will give you a bit bonus score (because I think 99% of the class did that). The works that are sent to email will be showcased in class for peer evaluation.

I suppose many of you would be curious what that listening test is all about, don't worry about that, as long as you attend and complete it, you will get full score, the test is more like a survey in my opinion. I will not tell what actually it tests, just not to ruin the surprise (or maybe the topic will be different from mine).

Grading wise, as many people has commented is very LENIENT.
The mean for each assignment is around 4.3/5, not data on the SD, but the highest score is usually 4.9 to 5

I am above mean in all assignments, with 1 highest in class, and my final assignment is 0.07 points away from the highest. Got A+

comment_content:
Video and music editing course. You are given a topic every two weeks and you have to make/remix a piece of music (1-2 minutes) as a submission. It is optional to edit a video with the music but everyone does it. The topics are flexible, you can be loosely related to the topic and it will be fine.

Topics this semester are:
1. Avengers Parody
2. HKUST brand video spoof
3. Assassin's Creed Remix
4. Rewrite the Stars Rewrite
5. Moana Discovery
6. Final Project: Extended Mashup Trailer (same but 2-3 mins)

You area also required to provide a director's cut, which is an explanation video to show how you produced your work.
comment_teaching:
You mostly learn from your own experimenting and experience, just search online if you don't know how to do anything. There are also (optional) tutorials where the TAs teach some of the more basic techniques.

There's not much teaching because you can choose to use any software you want, so what you learn is up to you.

Most of the class time is spent reviewing other people's work to learn from them.
comment_workload:
The time you spend on it depends on how much you want to mix or compose music, and how many effects and cuts or sound effects you want to add.

Usually if you add more stuff to your submission you'll get a higher grade. But if you're able to produce a good submission without much effort then good for you. You could spend 1-2 hours every 2 weeks, or work for hours every single day if that's how you want to spend your time. The longest I've spent is probably around 23 hours on one submission for what I consider a masterpiece (it got really high marks).
comment_grading:
Andrew Horner is a really nice teacher and tries to give as many high grades as possible.

You get a base level grade for handing in your work to canvas. You can also send your submission to the professor for each assignment to get bonus marks (pretty sure everyone does this). Your submission (if you sent it to the professor) will be played in class for everyone to see (don't worry about it it's not scary). Then the professor will say something nice about your work, and move on to the next submission.

Grading is subjective to the professor, the TAs, and partly from the class. There is a canvas quiz each class and each student can give a grade to each submission. Your grade for each submission is calculated by finding the weighted averages of these.

The final project has a higher grade weighting than the other assignments but the topic is more vague so you can do almost anything you want.

I spent time to make good submissions so I got A+. I had a friend take it and spent around 2-3 hours per submission (so like 15 hours of actual work in the whole semester) and got a B.

There were classmates making professional YouTuber level remix... But it is not necessarily to make it that pro~ Anyway your efforts will pay off in the grade!
comment_grading: I put a lot of effort and time and remixing music with Logic Pro X and even compose new countermelodies added on top of the soundtracks. All assignments got above 6 out of 7. Attended all lectures and just went to one bonus experiment, got an A.

comment_content: Learn basic skills of video and audio editing. Not much knowledge required as you will learn the skills as the class progresses. Recommended for those who wish to explore their creativity and try different things. There are also tutorial sessions available if you wish to learn about new softwares for editing such as Audacity.

comment_content:

1 Presentation: 50%
No midterms or finals
Attendance IS REQUIRED for lectures but not tutorials but I dont know the consequence of not going to class.
**(the attendance taking STARTS AFTER THE ADD/DROP PERIOD so quite a lot of students dont know about that, they didnt come to class after the first lesson)

You are only required to hand in soundtracks of specific themes for assignments, BUT video will usually give you bonus points, so everyone is making a video after some time.

Then he will comment "a bit" on your work. (really just a few words) So it is a course for you to explore how u can mix up soundtracks and video.

QUITE A LOT FOR THOSE WHO HAVE NEVER DONE THIS BEFORE. But if you got friends or you have taken COMP4441 before, highly recommend this course to you.

comment_grading:
VERY LENIENT GRADING. You got 5/5 for every assignment you hand in on time so it depends on those BONUSES.

BUT FIRST, people who have already finished their "COMP4441 computer music" course will definitely have advantages over the one who didnt, cuz the themes are set based on those in COMP4441.

Since I didnt take any comp course in the past AND I stop going to the tutorial after the 3rd/4th week, my works are obviously less appealing comparing to those who got experience in this field. Also, I did my work alone so this may affect

I got B range with about 0.5 to 2 bonus points for each assignments. Got no extra point for recognizing the original pieces of music from others' works in class.
